signals.headline: Fed Chair Powell confirms tariffs are significantly fueling inflation** — The Federal Reserve has acknowledged that the
signals.raw: Fed Chair Powell confirms tariffs are significantly fueling inflation** — The Federal Reserve has acknowledged that the current tariff regime is already pushing up consumer prices more substantially than initially anticipated.[2][5] Powell stated that "a good part of it is coming from tariffs" in the recent uptick in goods inflation during early 2026.[2]

signals.headline: Current tariff rate hits 81-year high** — The U
signals.raw: Current tariff rate hits 81-year high** — The U.S. average effective tariff rate has reached 11.0%, the highest level since 1943 (excluding 2025).[3] Section 122 tariffs are set to expire after 150 days, which would reduce the rate to 8.2%, still the highest since 1946.[3]

signals.headline: Consumer price impact quantified** — The Yale Budget Lab estimates that if Section 122 tariffs remain in effect, consume
signals.raw: Consumer price impact quantified** — The Yale Budget Lab estimates that if Section 122 tariffs remain in effect, consumer prices will rise approximately 1.0% in the short run, representing an average household income loss of about $1,338 (in 2025 dollars).[3] If tariffs expire as scheduled, the impact would be 0.5-0.6%, or roughly $650-780 per household.[3]

signals.headline: GDP growth masks underlying tariff distortions** — While U
signals.raw: GDP growth masks underlying tariff distortions** — While U.S. GDP grew 3% in Q2 2025, economists warn this figure reflects tariff-related trade swings rather than sustainable economic strength.[1] Businesses frontloaded imports ahead of tariffs, artificially boosting the growth figure.[1]
